In that time, the NOPAT margin declined from -0.95% to -1.22%. In tandem, the firm’s invested capital turns, a measure of balance sheet efficiency, has risen from 0.9 to 1.05. The net effect of this is that its return on invested capital (ROIC) has eroded from -1.05% to -1.17%.

CSP Faces Large Headwinds

The first issue facing CSP is clear just the firm’s income statement: revenue has been in decline since 2022, at a time in which the industry is growing. This has been driven by a decline in hardware sales in the TS segment. The HPP segment has also faced a decline in revenue as legacy Myricom products reach end-of-life cycles. Given the thinness of their margins in the TS segment, revenue declines have meaningful impacts on overall profitability.

Secondly, larger IT solution providers are better resourced and can compete with CSP on price. The TS segment is evidence of this, with low-cost providers dominating the VAR market. CSP has to shift to a service-driven model in order to avoid pricing competition.

The Department of Defence (DoD) is responsible for about 1% of CSP’s revenue. Government contracts are important for stability, but they can be problematic because of the federal government’s budgetary constraints and procurement delays; increased competition from larger defense contractors, who can lobby and use their relationships with the federal government to get business; and, regulatory and compliance risks, which can make it costly-to-serve.

As discussed earlier, because of CSP’s size, it is hard for it to throw the kinds of money at R&D that can lead to meaningful innovation. Consequently, the company is likely to continue to be an innovation laggard. Failure to enhance ARIA’s capabilities as a result of this weakness could lead to a slowing in adoption.

Valuation

CSP’s intrinsic value can be assessed by capitalizing its TTM NOPAT by the weighted average cost of capital (WACC), 10.67%, and then deducting the value of the firm’s total debt, $0.08 million, which gives an intrinsic value of -$6.5 million, compared to a market cap of $196.82 million. That means that the market has given a present to CSP’s growth opportunities (PVGO) of $203.3 million. That is an enormous premium for a company that is small, faced with numerous headwinds, lacks pricing power, and has large innovation constraints. Unless the company’s cybersecurity product adoption rates and managed services revenue increase, and operating costs are brought under control, CSP is unlikely to realise the implied PVGO.

Conclusion

CSP has struggled for sustainable profitability as a function of its size, commitment to a low-margin business, innovation constraints, and lack of pricing power. It is dearly in need of a big shift to high-margin, recurring revenue streams. Without that, the firm will continue to struggle for sustainable profitability. Investors should take note and avoid the stock despite its recent positive price movement.

data.title: Indie Semiconductor: Riding The Wave Of Advanced Automotives
data.description:
Investment Thesis

indie Semiconductor (NASDAQ:INDI) is a semiconductor manufacturer that specializes in automotive semiconductors and software solutions for advanced driver assistance system. indie is one of a few pure-play automotive semiconductor makers in the industry, which is one of the fastest growing semiconductor markets with $131 billion total addressable market and 10% CAGR.

indie is not well established yet, but it is growing at a strong pace and establishing its niche.

I believe they are worth a look for a growth oriented investor because:

  • indie is successfully growing their business, and their gross margin is growing as well.
  • indie has been establishing an economic moat with technology (500+ patents) and a unique position of hardware/software co-optimization.
  • indie will enjoy secular tailwinds from increasing global regulation, as well a growth in the automotive semiconductor industry and electrical vehicle market.

indie's Path to Profitable Growth

indie's revenue has been growing substantially in the past several years, and their gross margin has been improving. Their revenue grew from $22.6 M in 2020 to $216.6 M in 2024, and it is expected to reach over $300 M in 2025. The gross margin steadily improved from 42.3% in 2020 to 50.4% in 2024.

They are continuing to secure and win OEM validations, and I expect them to continue to do so. In 2024, they secured Automatic-Emergency-Breaking program wins from three global OEMs, achieved functional verification of radar SoC with their lead radar customer, and captured lighting design wins at North American and South Korea OEMs.

In the last earnings call, management also mentioned their in-cabin user experience system is performing well across western OEMs including Porsche, General Motors, and Volkswagen.

indie is not profitable yet, and their operating cash flow is negative. However, with the continuing growth, I expect them to achieve profitable growth in the near future.

Establishing An Economic Moat

indie has been building technological depth and breadth to create an economic moat. They have been putting in the effort to create an automotive powerhouse both organically and inorganically.

They developed in-house innovative products of high speed communication, sensor interface, and wireless charging in the past couple of decades. Also, they acquired companies like TeraXion (Photonics/LiDAR), GEO Semiconductor (Vision sensor), and Silicon Radar (Radar chip design) to expand their portfolio and technological depth.

With these efforts, indie is on the path to become the best-in-class mixed modal system-on-chip solutions company, and they have the capability to co-optimize hardware and software in this system. Their technological advancement is well demonstrated by their 500+ patents.

This technological depth and wide-portfolio will provide an economic moat and high competitive barrier to competitors, and these will help indie to expand their market shares and get more Tier-1 automotive manufacturer customers.

Secular Tailwind for Automotive Semiconductor

Semiconductors and electronics perform a variety of functions in automotives that include sensors, processing data, storing information, controlling signals, and connecting to the internet.

Three megatrends that are driving the automotive semiconductor industry growth are 1) the increase of electrification of vehicle drivetrains, 2) the increased adoption of advanced driver assistance systems and automated driving, and 3) the increased demand for convenient and advanced entertainment systems. indie is well positioned in all three areas.

The Global Electric Vehicle market is projected to reach $1.1 T in 2029, representing 6.95% CAGR, and the unit sales of EV is expected to be 18.84 M vehicle units by 2029. Currently, the Asia Pacific region dominates the EV market share, with 51.24% in 2023, and the region will continue to grow.

The U.S. market is lagging behind for now, but it is expected to grow significantly in the future.

indie has a presence all over the world including China, Korea, India, Germany, United States, and Argentina, and they are well position to take advantage of this electrification of vehicles.

Drivers are increasingly demanding higher levels of safety, comfort, and efficiency from their vehicles, causing the global advanced driver assistance system industry to grow at a rapid pace. Also, more and more governments are mandating driver assistance technology to in an effort to reduce traffic accidents, further increasing the market.

The global advanced driver assistance system market is projected to reach $124 B by 2029 (16.1% CAGR). The system includes sensors, cameras, radars, and software. indie is capable of manufacturing all sensor types of advanced driver assistance system, and will ride high on the growth wave of the market.

And finally, in-car entertainment systems have been evolving for decades, but the advanced audio system, connectivity, video capacity, and integration in the recent years has taken driver's and passenger's expectations to the next level. Also, virtual and augmented reality is poised to redefine the in-car experience. Augmented reality displays may overlay navigational prompts on the windshield, or virtual reality systems may entertain passengers with an immersive experience.

The in-vehicle infotainment market is expected to grow 11.6% CAGR until 2028, and this growth pace will certainly add to indie's growth trajectory.

Earnings Preview

The automotive industry and automotive semiconductor industry struggled in 4Q 2024.

NXP Semiconductor (NXPI) reported a 9% YoY decline in overall 4Q 2024 revenue, while Automotive segment revenue declined 6% YoY. NXP management mentioned that the Western Tier 1 automotive customers had been dealing with a cloudy auto demand environment. They pointed out the declining automotive production in Europe and Japan was exacerbated by inventory digestion at Western Tier 1 customers.

ON Semiconductor also pointed out their 4Q 2024 results were impacted by lower global auto demand with slower EV ramp up in the U.S. Also, EV demand weakened in Europe with a 10% drop in new vehicle registration during 4Q 2024.

Against this backdrop, I am not too optimistic about the earnings results from indie later this month. While I believe indie has a strong growth perspective in the future, I don't think 4Q 2024 result will be great, and it will be likely a mixed bag. Even so, analyst sentiment is positive though with 5 upward revision vs. 1 downward revision on their EPS in the past 90 days.

Risk

As mentioned before, indie is not a profitable company yet. Their net loss in the past twelve months was $114.7 M and the operating cash flow was negative $68.3 M.

Even though I believe indie should be able to achieve profitable growth in the near future, the route to get there will be windy and bumpy. In the meantime the stock price could swing wildly, so the investor should be aware of that.

Also, they announced a senior note offering ($190 M principal amount with 3.5% convertible). The notes will be convertible into cash, shares of common stocks, or a combination of cash and share. The conversion of notes to common shares will dilute the existing shareholder stake and may bring down the stock price.

EV adoption in 2024 showed mixed signs of growth and stagnation depending on region. The Chinese market still leads growth and is expected to continue, while the European market has been seeing stagnation. The recent executive orders are expected to slowdown EV adoption in the U.S. It's nearly impossible to accurately predict the path of electrification, and this unpredictability will negatively impact indie's growth and stock price at times.

Conclusion

indie has been establishing their niche in the automotive semiconductor space, and their growth trajectory has been impressive. The automotive semiconductor industry will continue to grow at a strong pace for the foreseeable future due to electrification of vehicles, growth of advanced driver assistance system, and growth of in-cabin experience system.

indie is establishing their technological advancement and economic moat via both organic and inorganic growth. This technological advancement will help them win market share and growth.

However, being a relatively new company in this growing field, indie is not profitable yet, and not yet generating positive operating cash flow. Therefore, the stock price will swing wildly at times. Also, the EV adoption path won't be straight forward, and it will be impacted by politics, geopolitics, and overall economic condition. However, I expect indie to handle the challenges well.

I believe a growth oriented investor with a high tolerance for volatility should take a look at indie semiconductor.

data.title: CGDV: 2025 Could Be One Of Its Best Years Amid Earnings Growth, Cheap Valuation
data.description:
Capital Group Dividend Value ETF (NYSEARCA:CGDV) outperformed the broader market index by 18% since its launch in early 2022. Nevertheless, the fund appears well-positioned to generate one of the highest returns in 2025 because of its active portfolio management strategy of investing in fundamentally sound dividend-paying stocks with strong earnings growth power. Its key holdings from the industrials, health care, tech and financial sectors are poised to generate record-breaking financial results with a healthy year over year growth. Despite outperforming the S&P 500, the ETF trades at attractive valuation. Its dividend growth factor is also expected to contribute to the total returns. Therefore, I maintain my buy rating on CGDV.

Promising Stock Market Outlook and Dividend Investing

The S&P 500 delivered exceptional performance in the current bull run by producing 60% in total return. With the US GDP growth of around 3% in 2025, a steady decline in interest rates and a high double digit earning growth for the second consecutive year, I believe investor sentiment is likely to remain high in the market throughout 2025. Furthermore, as AI mania accelerates and tech companies are poised to deliver more than 20% earnings growth for the first time since 2021, growth category has the potential to beat the S&P 500 returns.

Although dividend investing underperformed in the current bull run, I expect a significant improvement in dividend stocks performance in 2025 due to the broadening S&P 500 earnings growth trend. In the past few years, majority of the S&P 500 earnings growth was driven by the Magnificent-7 and other large tech stocks. However, the trend is expected to change significantly in 2025. Dividend-heavy sectors, such as health care, financials, industrials, consumer discretionary and utilities, are expected to contribute heavily to the S&P 500 earnings growth. Moreover, energy, materials and real estate sector are expected to return to positive growth in 2025, which is a great sign for dividend category.

There are numerous dividend investing strategies investors can use based on their risk tolerance and investment objectives. Dividend investors with a low risk tolerance can rely on defensive investment vehicles while high yield and high dividend strategies involve a slightly higher risk. The recent performance show that investment vehicles, such as CGDV, offers one of the best ways to generate market-beating returns with a modest risk factor. Unlike traditional index-focused ETF, CGDV is an actively managed ETF with a focus on holding stakes in dividend-paying companies with strong market fundamentals and earnings growth power.

How Does CGDV Can Generate Market-Beating Returns?

CGDV outperformed the S&P 500 index since it launch in early 2022. The fund’s outperformance is attributed the management’s strategy of holding a stake in dividend playing-companies with strong fundamentals. Its portfolio also offers an attractive combination of large-cap growth and value stocks. Although the ETF outperformed the S&P index since its launch, it appears that 2025 could be one of the best years for shareholders given robust earnings growth power of its portfolio holdings. Earnings growth has always been a key driver of the share price upside and dividend returns.

CGDV’s portfolio is spread across all the S&P 500 sectors with a largest concentration in industrials, health care, technology and communication sectors. According to the FactSet data, the health care and industrials sectors are expected to be the largest contributors to the S&P 500 earnings growth in 2025. The health care and industrials’ sectors are expected to produce 19% and 13% earnings growth in 2025, respectively. Both sectors struggled in 2024 with a low mid-single digit earning growth. Therefore, it is highly likely that the robust earnings growth could help in rebounding their stock prices and dividends.

What’s more, the fund held large stakes in top-performing stocks from industrials and health care sectors. For example, RTX Corp (RTX), GE Aerospace (GE), General Dynamics (GD), Carrier Global (CARR), Union Pacific (UNP) and Uber Technologies (UBER) are among its top holdings from the industrials sector. RTX Corp, GE Aerospace, General Dynamics reported record revenues, earnings and backlogs in 2024, with expectations for a double digit increase in 2025. Whereas, Uber Technologies expects gross bookings growth of 17% to 20% in the first quarter after exceeded expectations with a 18% year-over-year increase in the previous quarter. Its Uber One membership program grew by 60% year over year to an all-time high of 30 million in the recent quarter.

After a significant underperformance from the health care sector, a big rebound is also expected in 2025 because of its blistering earnings growth forecast. UnitedHealth Group (UNH), GE Healthcare (GEHC), Eli Lilly (LLY), AbbVie (ABBV), Abbott Laboratories (ABT), and Amgen (AMGN) are the largest holdings in CGDV’s portfolio.

On the other hand, stocks from the technology and communication sectors like Meta (META), Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL), Broadcom (AVGO), Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT), Salesforce (CRM) and Oracle (ORCL) enable ETF to capitalize on the tech-driven rallies. Its stake in Meta, Alphabet, Broadcom and Microsoft also increases it exposure to the AI market. CGDV’s positions in defensive companies, such as British American Tobacco (BTI), American International Group (AIG), Philip Morris International (PM) and Coca-Cola (KO) helps in contributing to dividend growth and lowering the downside in downtrends. Overall, CGDV’s portfolio is well-diversified with exposure to dividend-paying growth and value stocks from all the S&P 500 sectors. In addition, its concentration in industrials, health care and tech stocks positions it to generate robust share price growth in 2025.

Besides a portfolio factor, CGDV’s other characteristics, such as valuation, dividends, expense ratio and liquidity, make it solid investment vehicle for long-term. Despite solid price performance in the past years, the fund appears cheap compared to the broader market index. A low valuation signals that ETF’s price has a significant room for upside. In addition, its dividends are also expected to increase in 2025 because of healthy earnings growth forecast. The fund’s expense ratio of 0.33% is also below the median of all ETF while the liquidity factor appears strong with $14.6 billion in assets under management and 2.5 million in average daily shares trading volume.

Risk Factors to Consider

Unlike defensive ETFs, CGDV carries a high risk factor. This is due to CGDV’s portfolio exposure to a large number of growth stocks with high betas. However, the combination of growth with value and defensive stocks lower the overall risk score, The ETF earned a negative A grade on a risk factor based on Seeking Alpha quant analysis. A high grade on a risk factor is attributed its low short interest ratio and standard deviation.

In Conclusion

CGDV appears to be a solid investment option for dividend investors seeking to generate market-beating returns while taking a lower risk. Its significant concentration in high earnings growth sectors positions it to generate one of the best returns in its history. The fund also offers an attractive entry point due to its cheap valuation and high growth prospects. A low expense ratio, healthy liquidity and moderate risk factors also make it a great option for long-term investing.

data.title: TSPY Vs. ISPY: Advantages Of Active Management In Options Trading
data.description:
Selling 0 days-to-expiry ("0DTE") covered calls is a strategy that has revolutionized the derivative income ETF space due to its ability to capture more upside and similar income as their monthly counterparts, albeit with less downside protection. Funds like Roundhill's XDTE have outperformed its underlying index, the S&P 500, in total returns since its inception while offering outsized distributions of 20%. Of course, XDTE has issues like not directly holding the underlying and distribution rates that have caused overall price returns to drop, which is why I had been seeking 0DTE income funds with more conservative strategies that offer a better balance of capital appreciation and distributions. The ProShares S&P 500 High Income ETF (BATS:ISPY) had been the 0DTE fund with the best balance of both worlds. It has a 9.21% yield and performance that barely lagged the S&P 500, so I had already rated the fund a Strong Buy twice. But that didn't mean the fund wasn't without its issues. ISPY's covered call strategy was rigid and passive. It was good in the sense that the strategy will be profitable nearly 90% of the time and it made the fund predictable relative to the S&P 500. The issue, however, is that ISPY's distributions are volatile, which makes it more difficult for income investors to manage their finances.

I had overlooked this issue in ISPY because there weren't any daily covered call S&P 500 funds that offered the same NAV sustainability and less volatile distributions. That was until I was notified of the TappAlpha SPY Growth & Daily Income ETF (NASDAQ:TSPY) in late October 2024. This is a fund that seeks to use 0DTE covered calls on the S&P 500 through a more active approach than ISPY but with less aggressive distribution rates compared to funds like XDTE. At the time, the fund was far too new for me to make confident decisions, but it has been about six months since its inception now. Data from TSPY so far has been great. The fund has shown extremely strong total returns with TSPY actually surpassing the S&P 500 since its inception. These total returns have been driven by a current distribution rate of 14.10% along with approximately 5% in price returns. Moreover, distributions have been much more consistent than ISPY due to TSPY's target distribution strategy. This is the fund that would be the ideal ISPY replacement and be a strong core S&P 500 covered call holding. That said, TSPY is still very new and we don't have particularly accurate assessments of how TSPY will perform in various market conditions due to its active approach. Its low AUM and TappAlpha's own limited history as a company are other risks to consider. Thus, I rate TSPY a Buy but don't recommend it as the sole S&P 500 covered call fund in your income portfolio or switching your ISPY position for a TSPY one just yet.

The Benefit of Active Management In Options ETFs

Although both ISPY and TSPY are considered actively-managed funds, ISPY's approach is much more passive. Both funds hold the S&P 500 as their underlying (ISPY holds the constituents directly while TSPY holds SPY) and sell 0DTE S&P 500 covered calls on 100% of their portfolio to generate income. With ISPY, however, the fund uses an index approach where they sell covered calls following the S&P 500 Daily Covered Call Index's methodology via swap agreements. This fixed formula has led to the fund typically selling its calls around 1% out-of-the-money ("OTM") and in my past articles about ISPY, I had discussed how this formula has been good enough because the S&P 500 only had increases of more than 1% in a single day about 14% of the time. In other words, ISPY captures the full upside in its underlying 86% of the time which is why we see ISPY perform almost just as good as the S&P 500 itself despite being in a raging bull market.

The balance of price returns to yield has also been phenomenal for ISPY. Price returns have been similar to that of GPIX but with consistently higher yields. Other funds like SPYI and XYLD do yield more than ISPY, but you can see how higher yields led to overall lower total returns versus ISPY and GPIX. This is an illustration of how selling daily covered calls has the potential to offer the same or higher distributions while capturing the upsides in the underlying asset versus selling monthly covered calls.

For quite some time, however, ISPY was the only fund taking a more conservative approach with 0DTE options. Other fund companies, notably Roundhill and Defiance, had their own popular 0DTE funds but there were NAV erosion issues due to outsized distributions. XDTE, for example, currently yields 23.80% and SPYT has a public target distribution rate of 20%. Both funds have seen NAV erosion issues and although reinvestment of some dividends can mitigate these issues, maintaining NAV is still key in any income portfolio.

TSPY is the first fund I came across that uses an active approach to selling daily S&P 500 covered calls without suffering NAV erosion. Per various interviews on YouTube with TappAlpha's CEO and Founder, Si Katara, TSPY has a target distribution rate of approximately 12-14% and aims to generate income while retaining capital appreciation. It's a very similar approach as that of GPIX and SPYI, but with daily covered calls. The advantage of this targeted distribution active approach is that the fund manager can be much more flexible with his options strategy. In various interviews, Si Katara has discussed how TSPY's strategy feeds off data and rules to set the strike prices of the options and do things like close out options positions early. The idea is that once you meet the target distribution rate, you don't necessarily need to take on more risk. Other times, it's worthwhile to take on more risk which can lead the fund to outperform other funds that are using fixed, static options strategy. For example, TSPY's call option is currently about 0.43% OTM. ISPY, on the other hand, is about 1.15% OTM. This shows TSPY's willingness to sell calls closer to the money and is the reason why the fund yields significantly more than ISPY.

Granted, the expiration dates are different, but historical options trades in both funds have also shown TSPY's willingness to go close to the money. TSPY's November 29, 2024 call, for example, was only sold 0.28% OTM when compared to the S&P 500's opening price of 6,003 on that day.

While selling options closer to the money is more risky, a good active approach manages risk levels and is able to generate higher returns than a fixed approach. And so far, TSPY has shown they're capable of doing this. Compared to ISPY, TSPY has generated similar or better returns despite paying out a much higher distribution rate 14% versus 9%.

TSPY's distributions have also been far more consistent than ISPY. Although we only have five months worth of distributions from TSPY, payouts have been very consistent with a standard deviation of only 0.85%. On the other hand, ISPY's distributions have a standard deviation of 10.44%. If you're not tracking the S&P 500 and volatility from day-to-day, ISPY can very much ruin your day with significantly lower-than-expected payouts.

TSPY and ISPY have also had primarily return of capital distributions estimated in their 19a1 notices. However, I do expect TSPY to be slightly more tax-efficient as they manage the options positions themselves in addition to the use of 1256 index contracts. As always, please consult a professional tax advisor for your specific situation.

TSPY's Risks

The elephant in the room, however, is that TSPY has only been around for about six months. Naturally, we have very limited data on how TSPY will perform across different market conditions. In ISPY's case, the data is also limited to a little more than a year, but the difference is that a static, formulaic approach makes it easy to predict its returns relative to the S&P 500. When volatility goes up, ISPY's distributions will go up and it's going to follow the S&P 500's price movements just with a capped upside. With TSPY, however, a key consideration will be how sustainable its 12-14%/$0.30 distribution target actually is. A fund like SPYI that targets 12% distributions, while not bad, has clearly given up more total returns in exchange for income. Performance from TSPY has been amazing so far, but it has only been six months.

In addition to TSPY's limited history, the fund company itself, TappAlpha, was only founded in 2023. While I'm all for taking chances on new innovations from smaller entities, the issue is that the company's business systems are unlikely to be as mature as companies like JPMorgan or Goldman Sachs. I do not doubt the TappAlpha team's business acumen, but this is nevertheless a risk in combination with TSPY's low AUM of $18M. There are liquidity concerns with this fund should something go wrong.

Don't Fully Replace ISPY With TSPY Just Yet

That said, TSPY's performance since its inception has been undeniably good. Its five distributions since its inception have been very consistent and supports its 12-14% target distribution rate. The fund has also seen zero NAV erosion which shows how its active approach captures returns more efficiently compared to ISPY's formulaic strategy. TSPY is a fund that I would like to make a core holding if performance like this keeps up which is why I rate it a Buy. Its limited history and low AUM does make it difficult to fully commit to this fund, however, which is why TSPY should still be held with other funds like GPIX, SPYI, and ISPY. But if TSPY continues maintaining its performance, it would definitely become the fund to replace an ISPY position.

data.title: WeRide: Do Like Nvidia And Bet Big On Robotaxi
data.description:
Authored by Jacques Georget

Investment Thesis

It’s time to cover this massive growth opportunity of autonomous vehicles, more specifically the world of robotaxis. This is certainly a hot theme nowadays. I’m sure that you’ve heard about ARK Invest and its front figure Cathie Wood. In their latest report ARK states that there might be as much as 50 million robotaxis operating globally by 2030. If that is true, the robotaxi players are set for a significant bull ride in these coming years.

So, in this segment there are a few players competing. Of course, we have American vehicle manufacturer Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA), but I believe that Tesla is rather expensive right now with a market cap of over $1 trillion. Also, you need to take into account that the largest untapped market opportunity for robotaxis in the near term will not be the US but instead China is taking the first place here. So, why not buy a stock with a strong foothold in the Chinese market, and a modest market cap of only $8.65 billion compared to Tesla’s astronomic $1.14 trillion valuation. That stock would be WeRide (NASDAQ:WRD).

I argue that WeRide is all set to capture a decent share of the market going forward, with a strong foothold in China, the geographic mecca for robotaxis. Maybe that’s the reason Nvidia recently invested in WeRide, apart from the obvious fact that the market for robotaxis is booming. I’m rating WeRide a 'Buy'. This is after the stock surged over 83% last Friday on news that Nvidia bought a major stake in the startup. The stock is now up by 81.35% over the last three months, but I believe there is plenty of upside left.

Strong Global Momentum

WeRide is a first-mover in this robotaxi boom, and the only tech company in the world with driverless permits in both China, the UAE, Singapore and the US. Moreover, the company has operations in 30 cities across nine countries, and actively working to expand its reach. Recent developments include WeRide launching the largest robotaxi service outside the US and China, this time in Abu Dhabi, UAE, in a strategic partnership with Uber.

Uber riders in Abu Dhabi requesting UberX or Uber Comfort may be matched with a WeRide AV for qualifying trips. The service will be available in key areas, between Saadiyat Island, Yas Island, and routes to and from Zayed International Airport, with plans to expand the operating territory in the future.

Furthermore, WeRide launched the first L4 autonomous robobus service in Singapore in July 2024, transporting clients between luxury hotels and resorts.

WeRide has also begun to penetrate the European market with Robobus shuttles recently deployed at Zurich Airport in Switzerland, and with eyes on the UK next.

Yes, a delegation of the UK government visited WeRide’s office in Guangzhou early this year in order to learn more about this futuristic AV opportunity and how the UK may integrate autonomous driving into its society. I expect UK to adopt AV solutions shortly.

Summarily, the momentum for robotaxis is strong and I’m sure that every government in the world is thinking of how they can integrate autonomous driving into their societies. This is because autonomous driving comes with many benefits. First, its a highly cost effective way of transportation with costs going as low as $0.25 per mile according to research shared by ARK. Secondly, autonomous driving addresses challenges such as high traffic accident rates, and aging and shrinking labour forces.

Robotaxis: a booming market opportunity

We all know that the market for robotaxis is huge, but still. The total addressable market is projected to reach 188.91 Billion by 2031, growing at a CAGR of ~60%, based on research published by Precedence Research.

Also, if we anticipate that there will be 50 million robotaxis operating globally by 2030, as stated by ARK Invest, the TAM would be even higher. Let’s run some calculations, assuming the following:

  • 50 million robotaxis
  • 15 rides per day
  • $10 per ride

With 50 million robotaxis and 15 rides per day and robotaxi we get 237.750 billion rides per year, resulting in revenues of roughly $2,377.50 billion. If that is true, and WeRide captures a mere 10% of the market, this translates into revenues of around $238 billion per year. Please note that this projection is very speculative, and that I will use a more conservative approach for my valuation of WeRide, but it shows the vast potential that companies like WeRide hold.

Nvidia just joined the bulls

I’m sure it did not pass unnoticed that Nvidia dropped Soundhound AI and Serve Robotics while buying a significant stake in Chinese WeRide. So, maybe we find the reasons behind Nvidia’s buy in the vast growth that the Robotaxi market is forecasted to uphold in these coming years. As mentioned, the robotaxi market is expected to grow at a CAGR of 60% in the coming years. You need to look hard, and I’m not even sure you’ll find another market with a growth rate near that of robotaxis.

Accordingly, it takes no rocket science to come to the conclusion that WeRide is set for a major bull ride, with multiple years of high double-digit revenue growth ahead. Another interesting aspect of Nvidia’s latest move is that the company shifted its focus from US-based investments to companies overseas, like WeRide and Nebius Group.

Summary of Growth Catalysts

There are several growth catalysts for WeRide, which I summarize here:

1. As a Chinese company, WeRide is perfectly positioned to grasp a share of the massive market opportunity existing in China. The country is said to lead in the adoption to fully autonomous driving and having a strong foothold in the country is a big advantage that I believe will help drive significant revenue growth for years to come.

2. Fully autonomous robotaxis are cheaper to operate than regular taxis, as you don’t need a human driver, with costs going as low as $0.25 per mile. This means that there is a financial incentive for customers to shift demand from traditional taxi journeys to robotaxis.

3. Although US and China remain the key markets for autonomous driving in the near term, with much of the demand coming from those two countries, I can see that several other countries are starting to implement regulatory measures, paving the way for autonomous driving. This includes recent regulations enacted by the German government for example. Germany was the first country in the EU to legalize fully autonomous driving without the need of a safety driver.

4. Having the world’s leading AI chip manufacturer as a major shareholder, that being Nvidia, is a big plus. It brings both recognition to WeRide as a company and emphasizes the strength of its business model, while providing close ties to one of its key hardware & software suppliers, as WeRide’s autonomous driving functionality is built on Nvidia GPU’s and Nvidia's AI software. Also it’s a rather big stake that Nvidia just announced, 1.7 million shares worth $54 million.

Financials & Valuation

For WeRide I expect revenues to grow by 100% in the first three years, in line with analyst consensus, while falling slightly in the remainder of the forecast period. This gives us revenues of $2,038 million by 2030. Furthermore, WeRide is boasting with very impressive gross margins. Its gross margin has been roughly 40%, give or take, during the last few years and I expect the company to maintain that level also for the future. This can be compared to Tesla who has a gross margin of 17.86% TTM.

For valuation purposes I’m using the P/S method as WeRide is yet to be profitable. I have compared the P/S ratio of WeRide to that of Tesla. Tesla currently trades at a forward P/S ratio of 10.22x. I find it likely that WeRide will trade at a similar multiple ones the company starts generating some serious revenue streams. I expect the market for robotaxis to remain a high-growth market for at least 10-15 years, justifying a high P/S ratio. Using my 2030 revenue forecast, we get a market cap of roughly $20.8 billion for WeRide. With 274 million in outstanding shares that translates into a target price of $76 per share.

Risks

As with all investments there are some risks I would like to highlight:

1. WeRide is not profitable. In FY2023 the company posted a net loss of $279.2 million. It's likely that WeRide will need to raise additional capital causing shareholder dilution, which may affect the stock price negatively.

2. The market forecast is strong but there may be a delay in the global adoption of autonomous vehicles as regulations may put spokes in the wheels for WeRide. Germany is the first country in the EU to enact on a self-driving regulation but many countries remain.

3. Tensions between the US and China may create obstacles for WeRide, who have operations in both jurisdictions.

Conclusion

I believe that invention of robotaxis is one of the biggest revolutions of our time, hand by hand with artificial intelligence. Autonomous driving opens so many doors. It reduces costs of transport while allowing humans to move more freely from place to place without the need of a driver. Still, it seems that the stock market is a bit slow, as I argue that this massive growth opportunity of robotaxis is yet to be reflected in the stock price. Sure, there was a significant run this friday as Nvidia's stake got public, but it only proves my point. For me, WeRide looks cheap at its $8.65 billion valuation. As such, I rate the stock a 'Buy'.

data.title: InvenTrust Properties: Why I'm Warming To This Sunbelt Star
data.description:
It’s been a while since I last covered InvenTrust Properties (NYSE:IVT), remarking on this newcomer REIT’s attractive property portfolio and exposure to e-commerce resilient tenants. While I did have a ‘Hold’ rating on IVT last time primarily around valuation, the stock has gone on to do very well for investors, producing a 25% total return that well surpassed the 17.5% rise in the S&P 500 (SPY) over the same timeframe.

In this article, I revisit InvenTrust Properties including recent business performance and outlook, and discuss whether if I’m changing the rating, so let’s dive in!

Why IVT?

InvenTrust is a shopping center REIT that owns and operates quality open-air retail properties across the fast-growing U.S. Sunbelt. At present, it has 68 properties, of which 97% are in the Sunbelt. 87% of its properties are grocery anchored, marking one the highest percentages compared to peers and sitting just behind Phillips Edison (PECO).

IVT operates in an attractive sector as minimal new supply dynamics that are well below historical averages are expected to continue. Plus, suburbanization trends since 2020, with many companies adopting hybrid work models continue to work in favor of IVT’s locations. The Sunbelt also continues to experience faster-than-U.S. average population growth. This includes prime markets like Austin, Dallas, and Atlanta which represent some of IVT’s top markets, as shown below.

IVT carries a strong tenant base with many highly recognizable names. This includes top 6 tenants Kroger (KR), Publix, TJX (TJX), Albertsons (ACI), H-E-B, and Whole Foods (AMZN), which are often the #1 or #2 grocery / discount players in their respective markets.

IVT recently demonstrated strong Q4 2024 results, with same property NOI growing by 7.1% YoY during the quarter, representing an acceleration from 5.0% growth for the full year 2024. This was driven by robust leasing trends, including 52 leases executed during Q4 with a blended (new and renewal) lease spread of 15.5%.

This compares favorably to the full year, which saw a blended lease spread of 11.3%, and reflects higher demand toward the end of the year. Leased occupancy grew by 40 basis points sequentially and 120 bps YoY to 97.4% and tenant retention remained strong at 94%. Recently executed leases include in-demand concepts such as Ulta Beauty (ULTA), Nordstrom Rack (JWN), Crumbl Cookies, Starbucks (SBUX), and Skechers (SKX).

Looking ahead, management is guiding for full-year same-property NOI growth of 4.0% and Core FFO/share growth of 4.6% at the midpoint of range. IVT is positioned to see a full-year’s benefit in 2025 due to eight properties that were acquired in 2024 for $282 million. This includes high-quality assets in Charleston, Fort Myers, and Richmond as IVT moves to become a 100% Sunbelt focused landlord.

IVT is also targeting ~$100 million of acquisitions, to be driven in part by potential dispositions in California to recycle capital into higher-growth Sunbelt markets. Speaking of which, the Sunbelt market is estimated to see 7% population growth between now and the end of this decade, comparing favorably to the 1% U.S. national average.

The property portfolio is also well-positioned to benefit from the dual dynamic of historically new supply, which contributes to NOI growth. As shown below, IVT has a presence in most of the top growing markets in the U.S., combined with a low estimated new supply ratio (as a percentage of total market) of 0.5% annually.

Importantly, IVT carries on of the strongest balance sheets in the shopping center space, with a high fixed charge coverage ratio of 4.5x and low net debt-to-adjusted EBITDA ratio of 4.1x, sitting far below the 6.0x level generally considered safe for REITs by ratings agencies. As shown below, IVT’s net debt to adjusted EBITDA ratio currently sits at the lowest level since at least 2021.

IVT also currently yields 3.0%. The dividend is well-supported by a 52% payout ratio and was raised by 5% last year. IVT’s safe payout ratio also means that it has ample retained funds to make acquisitions and developments.

Admittedly, IVT isn’t the cheapest of the shopping center REIT bunch at the current price of $30.15 with a forward P/FFO of 16.4. This puts it at a higher price point than Kimco Realty (KIM) and Regency Centers (REG), which carry forward P/FFOs of 12.7 and 16.1, respectively. However, I believe IVT is deserving of its valuation due to its focus on fast-growing Sunbelt markets as opposed to KIM’s coastal market focus, and due to IVT having a higher grocery-anchored property concentration than REG.

Plus, IVT’s comparably lower leverage ratio and dividend payout ratio means that it has ample funds to support future growth. This is supported by analyst FFO/share growth estimates of 5% to 8% annually over the next 2 years. SA’s Quant also gives IVT a ‘Buy’ rating with a score of 4.1 out of 5.

Risks to the thesis include higher interest rates, which could raise IVT’s cost of debt to fund acquisitions and development. Moreover, higher new supply in its market could compress rent growth. Lastly, a macroeconomic downturn due to inflation, tariffs, or federal workforce layoffs could negatively IVT’s tenants.

Investor Takeaway

InvenTrust Properties is a quality shopping center player with a high concentration of grocery-anchored properties in the fast-growing Sunbelt region. It continues to benefit from strong tenant demand, favorable demographic trends, and limited new supply. This includes solid Q4 results, which showed robust leasing spreads and a disciplined balance sheet, which reinforces its growth potential.

While IVT trades at a premium valuation compared to some peers, its strategic market positioning, lower leverage, and strong FFO growth potential justify the higher multiple. Considering all the above, I’m upgrading IVT to a ‘Buy’ for investors who prize quality, growth and a respectable 3% dividend yield.
